entry.title: New Teaching Statement
entry.content:
At the core of my approach to teaching is a firm belief that students learn by constructing new understanding through modifying, reorganizing, and adding to their prior knowledge. By taking this constructivist perspective on learning, every student comes into my classroom not as a blank slate, but as a person with unique experiences and useful knowledge from which other members of the classroom can learn. This sets the foundation for an inclusive classroom focused on active inquiry and exploration rather than passive transmission of facts. This active inquiry is focused on the construction of some sort of personally meaningful artifact–an authentic manifestation of the knowledge the student has gained during a course that is created by exploring something that is important to them. By asking students to engage with their own interests by using the tools demonstrated during a course, I show that course content is not knowledge for knowledge’s sake but rather a collection of actionable items that can prepare them for new learning opportunities in the future.

Teaching Experience

Teaching has always been a genuine passion of mine and was the key factor in my decision to attend graduate school in the Learning Sciences. As the first ever graduate of the joint PhD in Computer Science and Learning Sciences at Northwestern, the central point of my interest was the disconnect between expert-level knowledge and expert-level pedagogical practice – why does the former not entail the latter? Now as Assistant Professor of Instruction of Computer Science at Northwestern University, I teach some of the largest courses on campus. In the 2022-2023 academic year, total enrollment in my courses was around 1000 students or over 10% of Northwestern’s undergraduate population.

As a graduate student, I had a number of teaching experiences ranging from Introduction to Statistics (SESP 210) and Causal Inference (SESP 310) for the School of Education, to an interdisciplinary programming course, An Introduction to Multi-agent Based Modeling (COMP_SCI 372). My focus in each of these experiences was to help students develop a literacy in each course; where they left being able to see the world in a new way. I now follow this same process at an entirely different scale (about 325 students per quarter) in my COMP_SCI 110, Intro to Computer Programming. The course, in 10 weeks, takes students who have never programmed before and empowers them to not just tackle small computational tasks, but asks them to express themselves via computation. The course engages students by approaching programming languages as a set of basic blocks that can be combined together to make astonishing creations in addition to interacting with real-world data; all with a goal of seeing the world as computable and seeing themselves as capable of computing.

In COMP_SCI 111, Fundamentals of Computer Programming, the audience is quite different than COMP_SCI 110 and consists of Computer Science majors and other students from the school of engineering. One of the challenging aspects of this course, besides its size of 400+ students each Fall quarter, is that nearly 60% of students come in with some programming experience. This creates a tremendous skill-gap among students in the course. I tackle this problem in several ways: 1. Grouping students into Tutorial Teams composed of a variety of experience levels; 2. Supervising a staff of 30+ course staff; 3. Creating a culture of peer mentoring where students help each other learn rather than compete with each other; 4. Offering additional opportunities for advanced students to go beyond the syllabus topics and engage more deeply with CS fundamentals.

In my design of COMP_SCI 396, Communicating Computer Science, I aim to fill a large gap in typical collegiate computer science curricula: the study and practice of taking the technical topics and communicating those ideas with non-technical audiences. The course is centered on active learning with sessions focused on asking students to engage as critical consumers of existing media and as critical creators of new artifacts. The course culminates in a project, with examples ranging from a blog discussing the pros and cons of different programming languages, to a podcast on amplifying African languages in natural language processing, to lesson plans for K-5 students on the mechanics of machine learning.

Building a Classroom Where All Students Can Learn

In my classrooms, I use three strategies to build an inclusive learning community. The first is emphasizing peer learning. By establishing all students in my classes as teachers in their own right, students both experience teaching a topic to another student as well as learning how to evaluate how others teach. This is in purposeful opposition to the usual instructor-student power structure. In both my large intro courses, each Wednesday we have in-class Tutorial exercises where students are grouped together and work to solve a programming task. These days are flipped in that students watch a short pre-recorded lecture (and complete a low-stakes embedded multiple-choice quiz) and then come to class to work together to complete the task. During these workshops we actively encourage students to engage in paired-programming, to help each other past road blocks they encounter, and to practice communicating programming ideas to other people (a key goal of both courses). Programming is often construed as a solitary and asocial act and these sessions are meant to uproot that idea.

The second strategy is emphasizing self-reflection. One of the hardest parts of learning is knowing what you know, and knowing what you do not know. In my classroom, I try to get students to actively think about what they have been learning over time. Every Friday in my large courses we have Practice+ Days. Using PollEverywhere we, as a class, work together to first break down a larger programming problem into parts and then solve each of those parts – each one meant to serve as a “checkpoint” for that week’s topic. By consistently reminding students in my courses to consider how their thinking is changing, students not only track their own learning, but can also better identify where they need the most help. Thirdly, my constructionist approach to learning frames learning experiences as building a toolbox in order to better understand ourselves and the world around us. Learners can then use these new tools to explore a topic of their own interest. In all of my courses, including 110 and 111 despite their large class sizes, I include significant project components where students choose what to work on, asking students to challenge and question the world around them

Course and Lesson Design Strategies

When approaching the task of designing a new course, I follow a backwards design strategy: I first start by creating a series of learning objectives and only after I have decided on those learning objectives do I move on to the details of course timelines, activities, etc. For example, in COMP_SCI 110, I start with a given topic and then design an activity where students would need to both confront the theoretical aspects of the content and then actually apply the ideas to real data and situations. An important factor in my lesson design in this course was overcoming the students’ perceptions that they “weren’t computer people.” Each activity first focused on understanding the programming idea at work by showing students how they already understood this concept (for example, if statements in programming are simply branching logic structures that we as humans use constantly). Only after we ground the ideas in our experience do we move to the syntax of a particular language.

Unfortunately, computer science has suffered from systemic issues that have prevented many students from engaging in meaningful computational experiences. At the core of my course design is framing computation not as a tool reserved for the few, but as a way of empowering learners to challenge the world around them. A learner does not need to be a software engineer in order to to use computation to seek, explore, and challenge. Broadening participation in computing is more than just simply recruiting new students to the field. It also requires us to question and reimagine the ways we teach computation–to connect it to students’ lives in a way that they can see it as enabling themselves rather than as something that “other people” do.

Assessing and Improving My Own Teaching

Just as students, courses, technologies, and textbooks are constantly changing, so too should my teaching. While end-of-course teaching evaluations are certainly useful in knowing how to fix problems in a course for the next iteration, in my own teaching I have found it is important to understand how the students are feeling on a finer-grained scale. Whenever we introduce a new topic in a class, I do an informal secret poll. I simply ask everyone to close their eyes and then ask them to raise their hands on whether we are going “too slow”, “just right,” or “too quick.” I believe that this simple strategy both gives me a better understanding of how students are feeling about the content as well as making it clear that they have a role in guiding the class. Additionally, at the end of Practice+ Days, I ask students to submit short reflections (1 I Got It!; 1 Needs Some Work!).

Getting formal and informal feedback from other teachers and colleagues is also a valuable experience since each teacher approaches their classroom slightly differently. As a graduate student in Northwestern’s Teaching Certificate Program and as a Searle Center Teaching Consultant, I had the opportunity to both observe other teachers and be observed myself which I have tried to also incorporate into my teaching as a faculty member. Finally, as an educational researcher myself, I both stay current with the educational literature of the field and find ways to ask and then answer questions about student learning in my own courses. My goal is to be a responsive teacher who consistently improves on course materials, assignments, and my own pedagogy to create a supportive and inclusive environment for all students.

Future Pedagogical Work

In addition to actively publishing educational research focused both around my courses and research, I am currently working on two pedagogical related projects. The first is developing a new course in the Computer Science department focused on Pedagogical Practice in Computer Science Coursework. The course centers around the ideas in teaching computer science at the university level and is specifically targeted towards the Graduate TAs and Undergraduate PMs in our department that are looking for a way to more deeply engage with teaching. The first half of the course will be rooted in pedagogical theory with students working together to understand the past, present, and future of CS Pedagogy at the collegiate level. The second half of the course will be focused on a project that asks students to choose either a research or design pathway within the context of CS coursework offered here at Northwestern. Students might choose the research pathway with the goal of producing a short academic research paper on, for example, investigating students’ evolving perceptions of programming languages in COMP_SCI 110. A student in the design pathway might develop a tool for teaching assistants to keep track of student questions during office hours.

The second project is focused on identifying aspects of the current COMP_SCI 110 curriculum that might be re-imagined to better serve the growing student body in the course. From Winter 2022 to Winter 2023, enrollment in this class jumped 44%. While the course covers a large number of CS topics, there are many topics and fields of computing it does not cover that this expanding student body may be interested in. I will be working to identify those missing interests and designing ways of further engaging this non-major audience to become deeply involved with computing.
entry.summary: a new take on my teaching statement

entry.title: Spring Retreat
entry.content:
Today’s retreat was centered on Radical Hope. Unfortunately, I wasn’t able to make it to that portion of retreat because I teach in the mornings and then had to commute to the retreat downtown.

I did however get to hear about a bunch of other Fellow’s projects which was great. Arend and I talked a bit about the use of various digital technologies in programming classes (he teaches in the Data Science program so we have a bit of overlap). His course is large (70ish) but still project focused, so we talked a lot about how that scales.

Project Updates

I also got a chance to talk with Mike about my project which was great. I’ll be re-implementing the Peer Feedback assignment again in a few weeks. We discussed whether or not I should formally evaluate it and decided to leave it as a “game-time decision.” Since there were a number of technology related issues in the Winter quarter, I’m probably going to keep this as similar to the last quarter as possible just because I don’t have time to create the necessary technological solutions to totally solve the issues with Canvas Peer Review.

entry.title: Winter Salon
entry.content:
Today’s salon was centered on Distracted: Why Students Can’t Focus and What You Can Do About it.

Technology Distractions in the Classroom

This is a weird one for me. I can’t ban laptops in my classroom. They’re our main tool! Additionally, in classes like mine that are 200+ students, no matter the community guidelines we set…there’s always a number of students who choose not to follow those guidelines. My main attempts at mitigating some of these things come in the form of my class design. Monday’s are the only passive lecture day. Wednesdays, students spend working in groups on a shared programming assignment. Friday, we do an “active” lecture day where students work in pairs to solve shorter programming problems via PollEverywhere.

To incentivize working together and actually speaking to each other about programming anyone that attends and participates on Wednesdays and Fridays gets full credit for those assignments (with those who choose not to attend getting graded on accuracy). However, each quarter, a small group of students has emerged that does one of the following:

  • Simply waits for their group to solve the challenge and submits the group’s response as their own
  • Randomly guesses on PollEverywhere responses in order to receive credit
  • Just sits in the classroom without participating at all and claims “attending” should be enough to receive credit

Additionally, I’ve seen more and more students equate “attendance” with “understanding.” In meetings with students after exams, they often start with “I attend nearly every class” which is only the START of a learning process.

Aiming for Wonder

A big theme of today’s discussion was wonder and curiosity. In COMP_SCI 110, the target of my SFP, I’ve integrated a number of open-ended design assignments like the Digital Terarium Assignment. A lot of students have a fun time diving in and designing something on their own using the tools we provide them. It’s one of the times in the course where instead of fielding questions of “do we have to…”, students ask “how can we…”.

But this has also caused issues for me in this course. Many students see the terrarium assignment as “beneath them” (it even shows up on CTECs described as a “kiddie assignment”). While a small group of students see that the assignment can be as challenging as you want it to be (in fact…it shares a tremendous amount with the actual process of video game development), there’s also a group that fails to see that while the basic requirements of the project are just that, the real underlying challenge is taking an idea from your head, and getting to the computer to a point that it “understands” and “implements” that idea. It’s a process of taking a ‘simple’ action like I want my fish to jump in an out of the water and breaking it into programming tools:

  • First I need some way of determining the current position of the fish
  • If they’re jumping up I need to figure out where they’ll be next
  • If they’re falling down, I need to figure out where they’ll be next
  • How do I trigger the jump?
  • How do I make sure that jump doesn’t affect any of the other movements in my program
  • What sorts of programming tools can I use to make this happen
  • I can’t put it in a loop by itself since that would mean none of my other animations will happen
  • That means I have to integrate it with the other animations
  • …

That “small” action is actually quite complex. You need to be able to think with the tools we have introduced in the class in a flexible enough way that you can actually implement this thing!

This idea of “complexity is driven by the never-before-seen” is a common theme I see in intro classes. We’re so used to technology being so readily available and advanced, we never think about the dirty details. Thus, some students see me as pushing to understand “previously solved problems” that we could just ignore. But those problems are literally the goal of the course. We’re trying to see the world in a new way – which necessitates looking at the things we already knew about.
entry.summary: what to do when computers are your teaching tool

entry.title: Winter Retreat
entry.content:
Today’s retreat was centered on Picture a Professor. Now that I’ve decided on completing my Searle Fellows Project in the context of my COMP_SCI 110 course, I’m going to try to specifically reflect on some of the ideas from the retreat in that context.

COMP_SCI 110 is called “Intro to Computer Programming” and is a challenging course to teach because it’s:

  • the only course in the entire Computer Science Department which is majority non-major students
  • the only course CS course that specifically does not count toward a major or minor in CS
  • a large-format course (250 to 350 students per quarter)

While I do my best to present myself as a “guide” through the material towards a greater literacy, in a large-format course like this, it’s often hard to not only present myself that way…but to have students interpret my role in the classroom that way. In an effort to be as transparent as psosible, my syllabus is quite lengthy in this class – mainly to make sure that not only is there a “rule for everything” but that everything is written down for students to access without any need to inquire through me. That way, I’m purposefully removing myself from the loop. I don’t make judgement calls, I don’t stand as a barrier to anyone; instead, I’m only there to help you get past your learning roadblocks, while they syllabus is there to help you with timing, assessment, and flexibility if necessary.

Of course this causes its own issues in a course this size.

But back to the session itself. I found quite a bit of resonance in the idea of ritual as it’s an important part of my course design. I call it a course “rhythm” but it essentially encompasses the same ideas. In my large-format courses I follow a MWF course rhythm that looks like this:

  • Monday - Passive Lecture Day
  • Wednesday - Active Workshop (Tutorial) Day
  • Friday - Practice+ Day (Active Lecture with short activities)

I do this one because I feel that having a sense of “ritual” or “normality” in a class makes it more inviting. Secondly, this alternating between different modes of interaction (hopefully) makes it so that you can’t “sink into” any particular way of interacting with the course. I’ve never investigated the effect of this formally, but found that it kept people a little more consistently engaged (and perhaps equally importantly, helped prevent some burnout on my part – though it just shifts work from the weekday to the weekend for me).

One thing that I need to spend some more time thinking about in the context of this retreat is how do I show all of the work I do for accessibility in my courses in my teaching statement. It doesn’t really fit anywhere in my current statement, yet I’ve devoted quite a bit of effort to it so it should be featured somwhere.

Project Progress

After meeting with Mike, I’ve narrowed in on my project topic and implementation plan, but I still have a number of open questions for my project:

  • How should I evaluate the peer feedback assignment
  • Should I also include the narrativizing portion of the original idea
    • this involves asking students to “translate” a computer program into a paragraph their family would understand
  • How do I release the peer feedback guide for other people to use
  • What other design elements might be helpful to center peer feedback in the class

entry.title: Fall Teaching Salon
entry.content:
Today’s workshop was a meditation on The Norton Guide to Equity-Minded Teaching. We discussed a number of equity-centered topics focused on identity and belonging–both in the classroom and in larger academic communities.

However, the topic I most resonated with in terms of my project is the idea of defining the concept of rigor in the classroom. Our group spent quite a bit of time discussing this idea because rigor in science classes is often interpreted in a specific way. In these classrooms, rigor is almost always defined in terms of not only what senior faculty (“the field”) think should be covered in a class but also the way in which that material should be covered. In STEM classrooms, this often translates to:

  • Exams that emphasize memorization
  • “Low” exam averages (or “curved” exam grades)
  • The concept of “weed-out” courses
  • The overall idea that “if you can’t handle this you can’t be here”

All of these ideas are at odds with the concept of COMP_SCI 110 (the course in which I’m working on my Searle Fellows project). I don’t wish to assign students arbitrarily low grades. It’s not a course that counts toward the computer science major (thus can’t be a gate keeper course). I do not think that memorization is a particularly important tool in CS (though note that I think that memorization and internalization are linked).

My definition of rigor (in the classroom) is very much different than this. Frankly, I don’t think that rigor is a necessary term when it comes to learning. Challenge is a better word. Though I acknowledge this comes from my own educational ethos, I believe that challenge is a necessary part of a course. We shouldn’t take courses to be told what we need to know. We should take courses to challenge ourselves to go beyond what we can currently do. This, for each person, looks different.

In 110, we’re asking you to learn a new way of thinking. After all, computer programming is simply the act of translating what’s in a human’s head into terms a computer can understand. This, in and of itself, is a rigorous idea. To form a new literacy is an incredibly deep (not to mention authentic) task. Now, can we expect someone to become an expert in 10 weeks? No – but is expertise the standard? Are all the students in this course seeking expertise? Or are they simply seeking exposure?

I don’t have any answers here, but I do think at least the following:

  • Challenge isn’t anathema to fairness
  • Rigor can be accomplished without exclusion
  • Grades alone are not indicative of rigor
  • Course design can allow for pursuits at varying levels of engagement (rigor) for wider audiences

entry.title: Fall Retreat
entry.content:
While there were a lot of pieces to today’s retreat, I wanted to spend some time reflecting on two particular parts.

On Reflective Teaching

Kate led today’s session on Becoming a Critically Reflective Teacher. During my PhD, I studied quite a bit of critical theory and its influence on pedagogy, particular in the K-12 space. Though much of that though influences my education philosophy and course design, I don’t necessarily name it as such.

My focus in intro coursework (which is likely going to be the target of my Searle Fellows Project) is building literacy in a new field. While literacy in reading/writing is thoroughly defined, it’s not as well-defined in other contexts: math, physics, computer programming, etc. There’s this commonly held aphorism that you’ve truly reached fluency in a new language once you start to dream in the langauge. What does that look like for computer science? Does it have a parallel? Is that even a useful framing?

I don’t have time to answer those bigger questions at the moment, so let’s focus on some more specific implementation details in my course and my own teaching statement (the goal of the workshop).

Questions for my own Teaching Statement

  • I spend so much of my time on work that isn’t described or featured in my teaching statement: dealing with student issues; designing for flexibility; struggling with appropriate assessment. How do I include these experiences in my teaching statement in a way that shows not everything is perfect but I’m trying to make things better?
  • How do I show flexibility in schedule and content while dealing with the fact that students do not actually want flexibility in large courses – they want to know what/when in order to optimize their time?

Questions for Other Fellows / the Program

  • Are grades hurtful or helpful?
  • How do we deal with large scale classrooms? (100+ students vs. 1 professor)
  • Dealing with student expectations – what happens when students come into your class expecting something it’s not designed to be?

Guest Lecture: Raj Choudhury

Dr. Choudhury gave a guest lecture on the Scholarship of Teaching and Learning which I found very enjoyable. It’s always refreshing to see other people think about a problem space in the same way you do (even if they’re taking a totally different analytical approach). His example of an Major League Baseball game board vs an International Cricket match board is an amazing corollary to particularly computer science (the act of programming).

MLB Score Bug (above) vs an International Cricket Score Bug (below)

Programming is about “encoding” what’s in our brains, in a way that the computer can understand and execute. In a way, these score bugs are no different. They each encode a tremendous amount of information, yet, to an outsider, they’re inscrutable. A member of one community, regardless of their expertise in that community, could see a score bug (a concept with which they are quite familiar) of the other sport and be at a complete loss.

The process of learning is about becoming an “insider” or, in my opinion more appropriate terms, a community member. Programming is no different. Seeing a computer program without any knowledge of the “syntax” and “grammar” of that language would mean not having any insight into the underlying meaning of that program. Not to mention, this perfectly generalizes to other programming languages. Being an expert in one language is neither necessary nor sufficient to learn another language. Each will look different:

  • php
  • js
  • ruby 
  • var_dump('hello');

  • console.log("hello");

  • pputs 'hello'

But also, at their core, there are common features that we can look for (just like most spoken languages have concepts of syntax and grammar). And that’s what my intro classes focus on!
